The Teacher has reached a turning point in his investigation of life, and what he reveals is almost unbearable: death comes for everyone—the righteous and the wicked, the clean and the unclean, the wise and the foolish alike. In his relentless pursuit of meaning under the sun, he has discovered that outward righteousness offers no exemption from mortality, that goodness does not buy a person additional years, that wisdom cannot negotiate with death itself. This is not a comfortable observation, and the text does not ask you to find comfort in it. Instead, the Teacher calls this reality what it is: evil. There is something profoundly wrong with a world where the righteous face the same end as the wicked, where no person knows whether they will encounter love or hate before the grave claims them. Yet even in naming this evil, he does not despair completely—he recognizes that being alive, however briefly and uncertainly, remains preferable to death. A living dog, even worthless and despised, possesses something the dead lion no longer has: the possibility of awareness, of relationship, of experience.

From this stark assessment flows an unexpected prescription, not a grim acceptance but a measured celebration. The Teacher calls you to eat your bread with joy, to work with all your might, to love your wife, to anoint yourself with oil—not because these things will last, and certainly not because they answer the ultimate questions about existence, but because they are the portion God has given you in your fleeting days. This is not reckless hedonism; it is something more honest. He acknowledges the brevity and mystery of your time—you do not know the hour of your death, and you cannot predict whether your efforts will succeed or fail. The swift do not always win the race, nor the strong the battle. You can be ensnared suddenly in evil times, like a fish in a net. Yet knowing this, knowing the futility and fragility of all human endeavor, you are still called to live deliberately, to work earnestly, to love fully. This is the paradox the Teacher offers: embrace your assigned portion not because it is permanent or fully satisfying, but because it is real and it is yours, given by God's own hands.

The closing parable of the poor wise man adds a final knife-twist to the Teacher's reflection. His wisdom saved a city, yet no one remembered him or gave him credit. The calm words of the wise should carry more weight than the shouts of rulers, wisdom should matter more than weapons—and yet one sinner, one small failure of integrity, can destroy much good. Even when you discover what is true and right and see its power to deliver, the world does not guarantee that your contribution will be recognized or that excellence will be rewarded. You may spend your strength doing what is genuinely good and wise, only to be forgotten, dismissed, or undermined by forces you cannot control. This is the world the Teacher has examined from every angle. He does not resolve this tension or promise that faithfulness will be vindicated in your lifetime. What remains is the call: do what your hands find to do—do it with all your might, knowing that one day your hands will be still and your work will cease. The question the Teacher leaves suspended is whether you will live this truth or continue chasing the illusion that wisdom, goodness, or effort can purchase exemption from the human condition.
